description: Salt has a greater impact on flavor than any other ingredient. It minimizes bitterness, balances sweetness, and enhances aromas.

roles.salt: flavor enhancer, bitterness reducer, sweetness balancer

balance_logic: Salt must be added in the right amount, at the right time, in the right form. A smaller amount applied while cooking does more than a larger amount at the table.
contrast_logic: Salt contrasts with and cuts through sweetness and fat.
examples: properly seasoned soup vs. flat unseasoned soup

description: Salt needs time to diffuse into meat – season in advance (1 day for chicken, 2-3 days for turkey, 15 min for fish). Time, not amount, is the crucial variable.

roles.salt: dissolves proteins, creates gel that retains moisture
roles.time: allows diffusion and osmosis to occur

balance_logic: Earlier salting = more tender, more flavorful meat. Any time is better than none, but overnight is ideal.

description: Season boiling water until it tastes like the sea (2% salinity). Food cooked in properly salted water seasons itself from within.
balance_logic: The saltier the water, the more flavor the food absorbs. Most salt goes down the drain, so be aggressive.
examples: pasta cooked in properly salted water

description: Fat carries flavor, coats the tongue, and allows aromatic compounds to stay in contact with taste buds longer.

roles.fat: flavor carrier, texture modifier, heat conductor

balance_logic: The flavor of fat itself matters – good fat makes good food. Fat can be layered (multiple types in one dish).
contrast_logic: Fat contrasts with acid and salt.
examples: garlic sizzled in oil vs. water – oil wins

failure_modes.result: dry, flavorless, food sticks to pan

description: Fat creates five distinct textures: crisp (evaporated water + hot fat), creamy (emulsions), flaky (cold fat in layers), tender (fat coats gluten), and light (whipped fat traps air).
balance_logic: Choose fat type and temperature based on desired texture outcome.
contrast_logic: Multiple textures within one dish create interest.

failure_modes.case: fat too warm for flaky pastry

description: Acid balances other flavors – it acts as a foil to salt, fat, sugar, and starch. Unlike salt, acid balance is relative, not absolute.
balance_logic: Acid doesn't have an absolute threshold like salt – it can always be balanced with sugar, fat, or salt.
contrast_logic: Acid contrasts with richness and sweetness.

description: Acid keeps vegetables and legumes firmer, preserves red/purple colors (but dulls greens), and helps egg proteins coagulate faster but less densely.
balance_logic: Add acid early for texture modification (beans stay firm), late for color (greens stay green).
examples: red cabbage cooked with apple (color preserved)
poached eggs with vinegar (whites set faster)

failure_modes.case: acid added to green vegetables early
acid added to beans before tender

description: Use multiple forms of acid in a single dish: cooking acids (tomatoes, wine) added early, garnishing acids (citrus, vinegar) added at the end.

roles.cooking acid: mellows over time, transforms dish from within
roles.garnishing acid: brightens at the last moment, adds freshness

balance_logic: Cooking acids are subtle – their absence is felt more than their presence. Garnishing acids provide immediate impact.
contrast_logic: Layered acids create complexity without harshness.
examples: beef bourguignon (wine cooked in) + splash of vinegar at end
Caesar dressing (anchovies, Worcestershire, Parmesan, lemon, vinegar)

failure_modes.case: only cooking acid, no finishing acid

description: Gentle heat (simmering, poaching, steaming, braising) creates tenderness. Intense heat (grilling, sautéing, frying, roasting) creates browning and crispness.

roles.gentle heat: transforms tough to tender, preserves moisture
roles.intense heat: creates Maillard reaction, caramelization, crisp exterior

balance_logic: Already-tender foods need gentle heat to preserve tenderness. Tough foods need gentle heat + time to become tender. Browning requires intense heat.
contrast_logic: Combining both (sear then braise) creates complex textures.

description: Browning creates entirely new flavors that don't exist in raw food. Caramelization (sugars) and Maillard reaction (proteins + sugars) produce hundreds of new compounds, including acids.
balance_logic: Brown until golden/dark brown but not black. The edge of burning is where deepest flavor lies.
contrast_logic: Browned exterior vs. tender interior creates textural and flavor contrast.

description: Rest cooked meat before carving to allow juices to redistribute. 5 min for small items, 15-20 min for medium, up to 45 min for large roasts.

roles.loose cover: prevents cooling while allowing steam escape

balance_logic: Remove meat 5-10°F below target temperature; carryover completes cooking.

description: Temporary emulsions (vinaigrette) separate quickly and must be re-emulsified. Permanent emulsions (mayonnaise) use emulsifiers (lecithin in egg yolks) to remain stable.
balance_logic: 3:1 oil to acid ratio for vinaigrette baseline. 1 egg yolk per 6-8 oz oil for mayonnaise.

failure_modes.case: oil added too quickly to mayonnaise
